title: Private Ownership
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro

outline.point: I. The Bible Presupposes Private Ownership
subpoints: A. Deuteronomy 19:14 — do not move your neighbor's landmark
B. Five other passages in Scripture address moving boundary lines
C. Deuteronomy 27:17 — cursed is he who moves the neighbor's landmark

outline.point: II. The Bible Regulates Private Ownership
subpoints: A. The Eighth Commandment — do not steal presupposes others own what you might take
B. The Tenth Commandment — do not covet your neighbor's property
C. These commandments apply neighbor-to-neighbor, not just individual-to-state

outline.point: III. The Bible Teaches Private Ownership
subpoints: A. Agricultural laws and tithes assume individual ownership of land and produce
B. Government has legitimate taxing authority but must not drive people into poverty
C. Eminent domain is not forbidden but is frequently abused

outline.point: IV. Private Ownership Is Designed to Glorify God
subpoints: A. Psalm 24:1 — the earth is the Lord's; we are stewards, not ultimate owners
B. Acts 4 does not teach Christian socialism — Acts 5:4 confirms voluntary giving
C. Paul in 2 Corinthians 8 calls for equality through generosity, not redistribution

outline.point: V. Private Ownership Must Lead to Generosity
subpoints: A. Harvest laws in Deuteronomy require leaving provision for the poor
B. Proverbs 3:27 — do not withhold good from those to whom it is due
C. Galatians 6:6-10 — sowing to the Spirit by giving to the household of faith

summary:
In this standalone Sunday School lesson, Pastor Lyro argues from Scripture that the Bible not only permits but actively presupposes, regulates, and teaches private ownership — particularly of land as the primary means of production in agrarian society. Beginning with Deuteronomy 19:14 and its five parallel texts, he shows that moving a neighbor's boundary markers is treated as theft worthy of God's curse, which necessarily assumes that individuals, not the state, rightfully own property. The Eighth and Tenth Commandments further regulate what already belongs to individuals, demonstrating that private property is a biblical baseline, not a modern invention.

The lesson extends to the purpose of ownership: all things ultimately belong to God (Psalm 24:1), making believers stewards who manage Christ's resources for His glory. Far from supporting accumulation for its own sake, Scripture demands that private ownership flow into generous giving. Acts 4 is not Christian socialism — as Acts 5:4 confirms, all giving was voluntary — but rather a portrait of people so unattached to worldly goods that they willingly sold possessions to meet others' needs. Paul's appeal in 2 Corinthians 8 envisions not uniformity of wealth but a culture of generosity in which abundance supplies lack. The lesson closes with a call to pursue generosity as the God-appointed goal of owning anything at all.
transcript:
## Introduction

Today we have a standalone lesson. We should start a new series two weeks from today. Next week is the lesson of the month, so we will cover chapter five of *Trusting God*, and then two weeks from today we will start a new series.

Today's topic is private ownership. Why do I want us to talk about that? Because I want us to think about everything from a biblical perspective. Why do we believe that communism is wrong, or that very strong socialism is wrong? Not because we are fans of this politician or that one. Some of you may be. But that is not the reason we support private ownership — the reason is that the Bible teaches private ownership.

This is another attempt at what I think is the theme of my ministry: always reminding us of the sufficiency of the Scriptures. The Scriptures are sufficient for everything concerning faith and practice, and all of our thinking should be based on them. Even political and governmental systems — how you think about them should be informed by Scripture. The Scriptures do not necessarily prescribe one particular form of government in its entirety, but they do give us principles that we should follow there.

## I. The Bible Presupposes Private Ownership

Let us start with Deuteronomy 19:14:

> *"You shall not remove your neighbor's landmark, which the men of old have set, in your inheritance which you will inherit in the land that the Lord your God is giving you to possess."* (Deuteronomy 19:14)

It may seem like a random text, but it teaches private ownership. I want to show you that the Bible presupposes, teaches, and regulates private ownership — especially private ownership of the means of production, which in an agrarian society is primarily land. Land is what produces what you eat, what you sell, what drives commerce. This is not a blanket endorsement of capitalism as it exists today, but it is an attempt to think about all areas of life in light of Scripture.

This verse is not a command to preserve statues or flags or monuments. It is a command not to tamper with the boundary lines of other people's property. "You shall not remove your neighbor's landmark" means: Chad is not to grab his stake and move it five or ten feet into Adam's property and claim it as his own. Why? Because that is not Chad's property — it is Adam's property. And this assumes that Chad owns land that is not Adam's, and Adam owns land that is not Chad's. To take from one to the other is stealing. And you cannot steal what does not belong to somebody else.

In the case of the Promised Land, God himself set up those boundary lines. Each tribe received its own territory, and within the tribe they could apportion it by lot or other means. But the tribal boundaries were set by God. To move those lines is to express discontent with what God gave you — a direct attack on God.

### Five Parallel Passages

This idea of moving boundary lines is mentioned five other times in the Bible — two in the context of God's curse for doing so:

1. **Deuteronomy 27:17**: *"Cursed is the one who moves his neighbor's landmark, and all the people shall say, 'Amen.'"* This is in the covenant curse section — if you violate God's covenantal commands, this is one of the specific curses pronounced.

2. **Hosea 5:10**: *"The princes of Judah are like those who remove a landmark; I will pour out my wrath on them like water."* When the government does not respect private ownership — when the government itself is the one moving landmarks, taking from some and giving to others — it becomes the exact object of God's curse.

3. **Proverbs 22:28**: *"Do not remove the ancient landmark which your fathers have set."*

4. **Proverbs 23:10**: *"Do not remove the ancient landmark, nor enter the fields of the fatherless."* The Proverbs add the application: do not take advantage of the weak by seizing their property.

5. **Job 24:2**: Job describes the wicked: *"Some remove landmarks; they seize flocks violently and feed on them."* And the passage implies that God should punish them for it.

All five passages presuppose that land is owned by individuals, not the state. They assume it as common knowledge. They are already regulating something already true: private ownership is what God has instituted, at least for his people, and I think we can apply the principle to humanity in general.

### Questions from the Class

**Andrew:** Does this address eminent domain — the government taking private property for public use?

I do not think Scripture forbids that. There are examples of it. When you read the story of Joseph in Genesis, he devises a way for the government to purchase land from the people. Government ownership of some land is not forbidden; what is forbidden is the idea that only the government can own land, or that the state has exclusive claim to all property. The fact that government may, in the public interest, act with regard to land — I do not think Scripture forbids that. We also see in the civil laws of Deuteronomy that the local government is in charge of ensuring that flat rooftops have parapets so people do not fall off — so government involvement in regulating property is not unbiblical.

Having said that, it is awfully misused. For example: a church in our area has a 15-acre property spanning from 67th Avenue to Bridgeport Avenue. The City of University Place put it to a vote whether to install a planter in the middle of Bridgeport to widen the street. Citizens overwhelmingly voted against it. The city did it anyway, using eminent domain to take property from people to plant flowers in the median. That is clearly an abuse. But the entire interstate highway system would not exist without eminent domain. So the thing itself is not wrong; it is frequently misapplied.

**Jim:** What about adverse possession — where you occupy land for a certain number of years and then claim title to it?

In the context of the Promised Land, Scripture would be against that. I will also say that holding land purely for speculation — leaving productive land sitting idle for 30 or 40 years with nobody using it and no purpose being served — I am not sure that is the best stewardship of property according to Scripture.

## II. The Bible Regulates Private Ownership: The Ten Commandments

Other passages go further and regulate private ownership. If the Scriptures regulate something, that presupposes the thing is legitimate and proper. Can you think of a major passage that regulates private property?

The Ten Commandments. "Do not steal." Come on — the Eighth Commandment presupposes that you do not own the thing, and that somebody else does. It is primarily directed to your neighbor's property, not to the government's property.

The Tenth Commandment also presupposes private ownership:

> *"You shall not covet your neighbor's wife; and you shall not desire your neighbor's house, his field, his male servant, his female servant, his ox, his donkey, or anything that is your neighbor's."* (Deuteronomy 5:21)

Coveting is wanting something to the point of being willing to sin to get it. It is primarily directed toward the property of your neighbor.

The Westminster Larger Catechism, Question 141, asks: "What are the duties required in the Eighth Commandment?" It answers:

> *"The duties required in the Eighth Commandment are: truth, faithfulness, and justice in contracts and commerce between man and man; rendering to every one his due; restitution of goods unlawfully detained from the right owners thereof; giving and lending freely, according to our abilities, and the necessities of others; moderation of our judgments, wills, and affections concerning worldly goods; a provident care and study to get, keep, use, and dispose these things which are necessary and convenient for the sustentation of our nature, and suitable to our condition... and an endeavor, by all just and lawful means, to procure, preserve, and further the wealth and outward estate of others, as well as our own."*

A beautiful thing about the Larger Catechism is that when they treat any commandment, they always note that every commandment implies a positive duty toward others. We are not only concerned that our own property not be stolen — we do not want other people's property stolen either.

All the agricultural laws, the offerings, and the tithes also teach private ownership of land and means of production. So Scripture clearly teaches that private ownership is a principle to be upheld, preferred over state control, and lived out in our lives — including in how we vote and how we serve in the public sphere.

### More Questions

**Alex:** From these passages it seems like best practice for a Jewish family would be to keep the land in the family. Is that the idea?

Some of these civil laws are strictly tied to Israel's relationship to the Promised Land. In that context, they were not allowed to sell tribal land permanently — the most they could do was lease it, up to a maximum of 50 years. We can extrapolate the principle that private ownership is something to be passed from generation to generation. Proverbs says a good man leaves an inheritance to his children's children. But I would not press the strictly landed application beyond its covenantal context.

**Nick:** [On the question of hoarding] I would put that in the category of wisdom. Hoarding is not something we should do — the whole idea of stewardship runs against that.

**Nick:** What about property taxes? Are they against what the Bible teaches?

Taxes in general are not contrary to Scripture. The Bible presupposes the government's authority to collect taxes — Paul says, "Pay taxes to whom taxes are due." God commanded Israel to raise armies, and those armies had to be fed and paid for. The government has the right to levy taxes to fund what God has commanded it to do. The construction of Solomon's temple involved significant taxation. So the concept is warranted in Scripture, though it is often misapplied. Nobody should be driven into poverty by taxation — that would be contrary to the general teaching of Scripture. And the question of how much is appropriate is a matter of ongoing application.

**Nick:** I have heard an argument that taxes should not exceed the tithe percentage.

The problem is that when you factor in all the mandatory offerings and tithes in the Old Testament, the total mandatory giving for the people of Israel was upward of 40 percent. So governments today are actually doing reasonably well by that measure. I do not think the connection to the tithe rate really holds.

## III. Private Ownership Is Designed to Glorify God

Private ownership is designed to bring glory to God. All of life is designed to bring glory to God, and we must always keep in mind that God is the ultimate owner of everything. Psalm 24:1:

> *"The earth is the Lord's, and all its fullness, the world and those who dwell therein. For He has founded it upon the seas, and established it upon the waters."* (Psalm 24:1)

Everything belongs to God, and we are managing those things for him. All that is ours is to be used for his glory, not for selfish use.

## IV. Private Ownership Must Lead to Generosity

Private ownership must lead to generosity. Deuteronomy has several passages about the poor. When you harvest your field, you are not to do it exhaustively — you are to leave things behind for those who do not have fields of their own. This is illustrated beautifully in the book of Ruth, where Boaz is already practicing gleaning laws and even leaving extra for Ruth, directing her to the corner of the field where he has left even more than required.

Proverbs 3:27:

> *"Do not withhold good from those to whom it is due, when it is in the power of your hand to do so."* (Proverbs 3:27)

The primary context of that verse is providing for others' material needs.

Paul writes in Galatians 6:6-10:

> *"Let him who is taught the word share in all good things with him who teaches. Do not be deceived, God is not mocked; for whatever a man sows, that he will also reap... And let us not grow weary while doing good, for in due season we shall reap if we do not lose heart. Therefore, as we have opportunity, let us do good to all, especially to those who are of the household of faith."* (Galatians 6:6-10)

Generosity is the goal of owning anything. The apostolic church exemplified this in Acts 4:

> *"Now the multitude of those who believed were of one heart and one soul; neither did anyone say that any of the things he possessed was his own, but they had all things in common... Nor was there anyone among them who lacked; for all who were possessors of lands or houses sold them, and brought the proceeds of the things that were sold, and laid them at the apostles' feet; and they distributed to each as anyone had need."* (Acts 4:32-35)

Knowing the original Greek is important here. This was not a one-time act where everyone pooled everything. The imperfect tense indicates an ongoing pattern: as needs arose, those who owned land or houses were not so attached to their possessions that they were unwilling to sell to provide for others. As needs arose, they were selling things. It is an ongoing generosity, not a communal pooling.

Some people look at this passage and see Christian socialism — but the very next chapter corrects that misreading. When Ananias and Sapphira lied about the price of their land, Peter said:

> *"While it remained, was it not your own? And after it was sold, was it not in your own control?"* (Acts 5:4)

Before the sale, the land was theirs — completely. After the sale, the money was theirs — under their own control. There was no compulsion, no mandate. The problem with Ananias was not that he kept some back, but that he lied about what he was giving. Acts 4 is not teaching Christian communism. It is portraying a people so unattached to the things of this world that they willingly sold possessions to help brothers and sisters in need.

Paul taught this as part of everyday Christian living. In 2 Corinthians 8:13-15, he writes:

> *"For I do not mean that others should be eased and you burdened; but by an equality, that now at this time your abundance may supply their lack, that their abundance also may supply your lack — that there may be equality. As it is written, 'He who gathered much had nothing left over, and he who gathered little had no lack.'"* (2 Corinthians 8:13-15)

Equality in Paul's vocabulary is not uniformity of wealth — it is everyone helping when the opportunity arises. This passage actually acknowledges that some will be richer than others in the church, and that is fine. But do not be too proud to accept help when you need it, and do not be so tight with your money that you fail to help those who do need it. He quotes the manna law to prove his point: the one who gathered much had nothing left over, and the one who gathered little had no lack.

Larry Burkett, who was a pioneering teacher of biblical personal finance, said: "Without a surplus, little could be done in God's work." It is actually good for Christians to have abundant resources — because then they can give abundantly. When our attitudes become controlled by our possessions rather than by God's Word, we are no longer useful to God.

## Conclusion

So: the Scriptures teach private ownership. Private ownership is designed for God's glory. And private ownership must lead to generosity. That is the goal of owning anything at all.
